coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent composites united metal-organic frameworks sensing system for detection of methyl-paraoxon

coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shaoyang
Luo, Mai
Liang, Huizhen
Zou, Liang
Cao, Jiliang
Wei, Jinchao
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Organophosphorus pesticide residues pose significant risks to human health, but conventional analytical methods are costly and complex. To address this, we developed a new environmentally friendly technique using a simple hydrothermal method to create a ratiometric fluorescent composite (RFC) with two distinct signals: blue and orange. This RFC allowed for rapid detection and quantitative analysis of organophosphorus pesticides. The composite incorporated a cerium-based metal–organic framework (Ce-UiO-66), which could degrade methyl paraoxon (MP) due to the two oxidation states of cerium. The resulting degradation product, p-nitrophenol (p-NP), specifically quenched the blue fluorescence of the RFC via the inner filter effect (IFE), while the orange fluorescence remained unaffected, ensuring consistency under microenvironmental and spectrometer conditions. This innovative approach was sensitive, with a detection limit of 0.55 μM, selective, and repeatable, offering a new strategy for pesticide analysis and safety evaluation of edible and medicinal plant and water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual mode
Nanozyme
Carbon dots
Metal nanoclusters
Organophosphorus pesticides

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of methyl-paraoxon in real sample
Determination of methyl-paraoxon in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CuNCs, displaying their cluster-like morphology. (B) TEM image of N-CQDs, with the inset showing a high-resolution image indicating the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. (C) Excitation (olive) and emission (orange) spectra of CuNCs with the emission at 621 nm and excitation at 425 nm. (D) Excitation (cyan) and emission (blue) spectra of CuNCs with the emission at 421 nm and excitation at 331 nm. Both spectra were obtained using excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) The fluorescence quenching rate of RFC to 200 μM p-NP at different amount of CuNCs. (B) The fluorescence quenching rate of RFC to 200 μM p-NP at different pH (10 mg Ce-UiO-66, 50 °C and 30 min). (C) The effects of the amount of Ce-UiO-66 on the absorbance changes of p-NP at 400 nm (pH 10, 50 °C, and 30 min). (D) The effects of incubation temperature on the absorbance changes of p-NP at 400 nm (5 mg Ce-UiO-66, pH 10 and 30 min). (E) The effects of incubation time on the absorbance changes of p-NP at 400 nm (5 mg Ce-UiO-66, pH 10 and 65 °C). (F) UV–vis spectrum of p-NP (gray shadow) and fluorescence spectra of RFC with 0 and 200 μM p-NP. (Inset in Fig.F: Photographs of RFC under UV light before adding p-NP (A) and after adding 1 mM p-NP (B)). The excitation wavelength (λex) is 350 nm, with both excitation and emission slit widths set at 5 nm.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of RFC with different concentrations of MP. The color gradient from dark blue to light blue indicates increasing MP concentration from 0 to 100 μM. (B) Linear regression of the quenching rate of RFC with MP concentrations ranging from 1 to 100 μM, showing a consistent decrease in fluorescence intensity. (C) Fluorescent quenching rate of RFC with the addition of 80 μM MP compared to different types of pesticides, indicating the selectivity of RFC towards MP. (D) Fluorescent quenching rate of RFC with the addition of 80 μM MP compared to various common ions, further demonstrating the specificity of RFC for MP detection. The λex is 350 nm, with both excitation and emission slit widths set at 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Smartphone images showing the color change of the sample in the presence of varying concentrations of MP, ranging from a blank control (0 μM) to 800 μM, with the color gradient indicating increasing concentration. (B) Smartphone images displaying the response of the sample to different types of organophosphorus pesticides, demonstrating the specificity of the color change to MP among other pesticides tested.

Schematic representation for MP detection using RFC. (The rightmost part of this figure shows the synthesis process of Ce-UiO-66, where the structure is formed using the raw materials Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6 and BDC under specific conditions. It also illustrates the process of Ce-UiO-66 degrading MP, where MP might originate from edible plants such as Ginseng and Coicis Semen. The far left shows the synthesis process of N-CQDs. The bottom part displays the synthesis process of CuNCs. The middle section shows the changes in the fluorescence spectra of RFC before and after the addition of the analyte.)

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent composites united metal-organic frameworks sensing system for detection of methyl-paraoxon

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Chengdu University, Chengdu 610106, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organophosphorus pesticide residues pose significant risks to human health, but conventional analytical methods are costly and complex. To address this, we developed a new environmentally friendly technique using a simple hydrothermal method to create a ratiometric fluorescent composite (RFC) with two distinct signals: blue and orange. This RFC allowed for rapid detection and quantitative analysis of organophosphorus pesticides. The composite incorporated a cerium-based metal–organic framework (Ce-UiO-66), which could degrade methyl paraoxon (MP) due to the two oxidation states of cerium. The resulting degradation product, p-nitrophenol (p-NP), specifically quenched the blue fluorescence of the RFC via the inner filter effect (IFE), while the orange fluorescence remained unaffected, ensuring consistency under microenvironmental and spectrometer conditions. This innovative approach was sensitive, with a detection limit of 0.55 μM, selective, and repeatable, offering a new strategy for pesticide analysis and safety evaluation of edible and medicinal plant and water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the use of pesticides has significantly increased to meet the growing demands of agriculture and pest control. However, with their extensive usage, concerns about their potential negative impacts on human health and the environment have also heightened. Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are commonly used due to their effectiveness in targeting the nervous systems of insects and pests. Unfortunately, their mechanism of action also makes them hazardous to humans and other non-target organisms [1]. OPs exert their deleterious effects on human health predominantly by inhibiting the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [2]. This inhibition results in the accumulation of acetylcholine, which in turn leads to the overstimulation of cholinergic receptors, manifesting as signs of cholinergic toxicity. The perturbation of this biochemical pathway can precipitate a spectrum of adverse health outcomes, encompassing neurotoxic disorders, developmental neurotoxicity, and a range of acute and chronic poisoning episodes [3]. Given the potential risks associated with OPs, developing efficient and sensitive detection systems has become imperative to ensure public health and safety. Conventional techniques for the detection of OPs, including gas chromatography (GC) and liquid chromatography (LC), are characterized by their substantial equipment expenses and intricate procedural requirements.

Current rapid detection methods for pesticides include colorimetric techniques, fluorescence spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, etc [4–6]. Due to its affordability, high sensitivity, and efficiency, fluorescence spectroscopy is extensively employed [7–13]. With the aid of nanozymes, a labeled fluorescent probe has been synthesized and designed for the highly sensitive detection of a range of organophosphorus pesticides [14]. Similarly, a new fluorescent probe has been employed for the detection of organophosphorus in fruit juice, characterized by its high sensitivity and minimal detection threshold [15]. Common single-signal fluorescence methods are constrained by instrumental inaccuracies, fluctuations in light source intensity, environmental alterations, and sample matrix effects [16]. The ratiometric fluorescence method, by incorporating a reference signal for internal calibration, surmounts these limitations inherent to single-signal methods. This enhancement elevates precision, sensitivity, and dependability, thereby facilitating more accurate quantitative analyses [17–20]. For instance, an ingenious dual-emission composite was designed as a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of mercury ions (Hg2+). The sensor has two emission peaks and strong anti-interference ability [21]. Similarly, a ratiometric fluorescent probe was developed using a Zr-tetraphenylporphyrin tetrasulfonic acid hydrate metal–organic framework, which demonstrates high selectivity and accuracy in detecting Cu2+ [22]. Dou et al. designed a fluorescence ratio NMOFs-Aptasensor that can be applied to the detection of Aflatoxins B1 under complex conditions [23]. To address the challenges inherent in pesticide detection, this study employed ratiometric fluorescence detection techniques for the analysis of OPs.

In the present study, a ratiometric fluorescent composite (RFC) was developed, comprising copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The luminescence mechanism of this composite was demonstrated to be fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Methyl-paraoxon (MP) was then selected as a representative OP to evaluate the applicability of sensing system, as it poses a significant threat to human life safety [24]. Our investigations revealed that RFC exhibited a notable sensing capability for p-NP, a degradation product of MP via pre-treatment of Ce-UiO-66. Currently, there are no specific studies on the systemic toxicity of Ce-UiO-66 in vivo or in vitro. Its use in colorimetric sensing and platelet aggregation inhibition indicates minimal harm [25]. Additionally, its role in water purification and insolubility suggests low toxicity [26], with a greater benefit in pollutant removal and catalytic applications. The implementation of the ratiometric approach significantly enhanced the sensitivity of the method and demonstrated promising recovery rates in real samples. Furthermore, this research also explored the potential for visual detection, facilitating direct observation of the detection process. Overall, this research corroborated the benefits of the ratiometric detection approach and introduced an innovative strategy for the fluorescent sensing of OPs.

Copper nitrate (Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O) was purchased from Beijing J&K Scientific Co., Ltd. Ethanol was acquired from Beijing Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 2,3,5,6-Tetrafluorothiophenol (TFTP, 98 %) was acquired from Shanghai TCI Development Co., Ltd. Citric acid was acquired from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory. Ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH) was acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. P-phthalic acid and ammonium cerium nitrate (Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6 were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was acquired from Shanghai Merrill Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. MP was acquired from American Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained with a FEI Talos F200X transmission electron microscope from Thermo Fisher Scientific in Massachusetts, USA. The Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) image was taken using a German Bruker Dimension ICON AFM. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were acquired using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Zeiss Sigma, Germany). The process of sample preparation included the application of droplets onto Cu grids (200 mesh, coated with carbon film), followed by solvent evaporation in ambient conditions. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were scanned by the Escalab 250Xi Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker, USA). UV–visible absorption spectra were captured using a DR 6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer from HACH in Colorado, USA. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Lumina fluorescence spectrometer and Infrared (IR) spectra were recorded with Nicolet iS10 IR spectrometer, also from Thermo Fisher Scientific in Massachusetts, USA. Sample images were taken with an iPhone 8 Plus (A1864/A1898) smartphone.

RFC was synthesized via a simple solvothermal method. Initially, a solution was created by dissolving 500 μL of Cu(NO3)2⋅3H2O (100 mM) in 49.5 mL of ethanol. Then, 1 mL of TFTP (1.425 M) was added to this solution, resulting in a yellow turbid solution. The mixture was incubated in a water bath maintained at 25 °C and stirred gently for a duration of 30 min. Following this, a yellow precipitate formed, which was subsequently isolated through centrifugation. The precipitate was washed repeatedly with ethanol to remove impurities. Finally, CuNCs were dispersed in ethanol ready for later use [27]. N-CQDs were synthesized through a straightforward modification of the method described in a previous report [28]. Initially, 200 mg of citric acid and 200 μL of ammonium hydroxide were mixed and vigorously stirred in water until the reagents were fully dissolved. Subsequently, this solution was transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and was incubated at 190 °C for a period of 10 h. Upon completion of the reaction, a brown-colored stock solution was obtained. Following the cooling of the solution to room temperature, centrifugation was used to separate the supernatant containing N-CQDs from the undissolved impurities. Finally, 8 mL of CuNCs (130 μg/mL) and 2 mL of N-CQDs (2 mg/mL) were placed into a round-bottomed flask and were stirred thoroughly to obtain 10 mL of RFC, which was used for subsequent detection.

The synthesis of Ce-UiO-66 followed a published procedure with minor modifications [29]. First, 70.8 mg of BDC was dissolved in 2.5 mL of DMF and then mixed with 800 μL of Ce(NH4)2(NO3)6 in a reactor. The mixture was agitated in an oil bath at 100 °C for 30 min, with the process involving stirring in water. After the reaction, a solid formed, which was collected and washed with DMF. The resultants were then further washed with ethanol and separated by centrifugation. The acquired precipitate was first dried under vacuum at 70 °C for 1 h, followed by heating at 100 °C for 16 h to guarantee the total elimination of any remaining solvent.

Incorporated 5 mg of Ce-UiO-66 into a 3.6 mL T-ris buffer solution (10 mM, pH 10), and subsequently introduced 400 μL of sample solutions with varying concentrations of MP into the mixture. The mixture was then incubated at 65 °C for 45 min, followed by centrifugation at 10,000g for 3 min to isolate the degradation product p-NP. After mixing 500 μL supernatant and 500 μL RFC in a centrifuge tube, immediately placed the sample into a cuvette and then proceeded to measure the fluorescence intensity at wavelengths of 415 nm and 615 nm. Fluorescence assessments were performed with an excitation wavelength set at 350 nm, and the emission slit width was maintained at 3 nm to facilitate precise detection of MP in diverse samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of methyl-paraoxon in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the potential of the well-prepared RFC for detecting MP in edible plant and water samples, including Coicis Semen, Ginseng Radix et Rhizoma, and tap water. All of the samples were sourced from Macau, China. The procedure for sample treatment was mostly consistent with methods established in previous research with minor modifications [30–32]. First, 3 g of edible plant samples was weighed and mixed with different concentrations of MP in a centrifuge tube. Then, 40 mL of ethyl acetate was transferred into the tube for pesticide extraction. Extraction was facilitated through an ultrasonic ice water bath. Subsequently, the resulting mixture underwent centrifugation at 4000 rpm to isolate any solid impurities. 20 mL of the supernatant was subjected to nitrogen blowing to remove the solvent. Finally, the resulting residue was dissolved in T-ris buffer solution for storage. For the processing method of tap water samples, 40 mL of tap water was taken from the campus faucet and filtered through a 0.22 μm micropore filter membrane to remove solid impurities. Then, 20 mL of supernatant was placed under a nitrogen purge to remove solvent. Finally, the obtained residue was redissolved in a T-ris buffer solution and stored.

5 mg of Ce-UiO-66 was added to 3.6 mL of T-ris buffer solution (10 mM, pH 10), followed by the addition of 400 μL of sample solutions with varying concentrations of MP. The mixture was incubated at 65 °C for 45 min and then centrifuged at 10,000g for 3 min. Subsequently, 300 μL of the supernatant was transferred to a 96-well plate. The samples were imaged using an iPhone 8, and the images were analyzed with the software “Image J” by reading the color information of each well.

The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized using SEM, TEM, X-RD, XPS, infrared (IR) spectrometer, fluorescent spectrometer, UV spectrometer and other techniques. As illustrated in Fig. 1
A, the TEM image revealed the synthesized CuNCs were dispersed in ethanol, forming clusters. In the Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) of CuNCs, signals of copper (Cu), carbon (C), oxygen (O), and sulfur (S) elements were detected, as depicted in Fig. S1 . As depicted in Fig. S2
A and B, the spectrum of Cu 2p indicated that the peaks at 953.1 eV and 933.3 eV were attributable to the Cu (0)/Cu (I) electrons of Cu (2p3/2) and Cu (2p1/2). No characteristic satellite peaks were observed near 942.0 eV, indicating that Cu (II) was completely reduced, consistent with previous reports [32–35]. As shown in Fig. 1 B and Fig. S3
A, N-CQDs were evenly dispersed in water with a particle size in the range of 2–4 nm and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. As depicted in Fig. S3
B, in accordance with the IR findings, characteristic peaks at 1230 cm−1, 1398 cm−1, 1568 cm−1, 1678 cm−1, and 3191 cm−1 were detected, which were assigned to CN, OCNH, CN, CC, NH/OH, respectively [28]. As depicted in Fig. S4
A, according to the XPS spectrum analysis of N-CQDs, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks were detected. To delve deeper into observation, peak fitting analysis was separately conducted on the high-resolution images of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. As the Fig. S4
B showed, C 1s was composed of CC/CC (284.8 eV), CN (285.4 eV) and CO (288.4 eV) bonds [36,37]. As shown in Fig. S4
C, N 1s was revealed that there were CNC (400.3 eV) and CNH (401.4 eV) bonds [36]. Furthermore, within the O 1s spectra ( Fig. S4
D), the peaks at 531.3 eV and 532.9 eV were identified as corresponding to CO and COH/COC, respectively [37,38]. The XRD of the reported N-CQDs was shown in Fig. S5
A, the weak and broad peaks suggest an amorphous structure for N-CQDs, aligning with earlier reports [39,40]. The XRD pattern showed a characteristic broad peak around 25°, indicative of the graphitic structure, confirming the crystalline nature of the material [41] As depicted in Fig. S5
B and C, carbon dots were clearly observed in the AFM images with a thickness of approximately 1.5 nm. Based on calculations, these carbon dots were composed of five layers of graphene. Moreover, various techniques were employed for the characterization of the synthesized Ce-UiO-66. TEM image analysis revealed that the diameter of Ce-UiO-66 particles range from 20 to 30 nm ( Fig. S6
A and B). The XRD of the reported Ce-UiO-66 was shown in Fig. S6
C, which exhibits a main peak at a 2θ angle of 7.3° and two characteristic peaks at a 2θ angle of 8.4° and 25.6°, which were aligned well with the characteristics of the previous report [42]. Examination of Fig. S7
A indicated that these particles possessed a spherical morphology with a notably rough surface texture. Furthermore, the distribution of the C, Ce, and O elements in the nanomaterials could be observed in the EDS analysis of SEM images ( Fig. S7
B). As depicted in Fig. S7
C, in accordance with the IR findings, characteristic peaks at 587 cm−1, 1382 cm−1, 1565 cm−1 and 1649 cm−1 were detected, which were assigned to CeO, CO, CC, CO, respectively [43]. As depicted in Fig. S8
A, based on the XPS spectrum analysis of Ce-UiO-66, C 1s, O 1s and Ce 1s peaks were detected. As illustrated in Fig. S8
B, the Ce 3d spectra of Ce-UiO-66 exhibited well-defined peaks at 917.8 eV, 908.6 eV, 902.6, 888.5 eV and 886.6 eV, corresponding to Ce (IV), further confirming the oxidation state. Significantly, the detection of additional peaks at 903.9 eV, 899.5 eV and 885.5 eV suggested the simultaneous presence of Ce (III). This manifestation of two oxidation states implied that the empty 4f band in Ce was of a low energy level [44,45]. Within the framework of Ce-UiO-66, the change in oxidation state, characterized by the electron loss, was evident as Ce (IV) transitioned to Ce (III), emphasizing the dynamic nature of the redox behavior within the Ce-UiO-66 framework. Overall, the characterization data supported the successful synthesis and structural features of the CuNCs, N-CQDs and Ce-UiO-66 in this study.

For optical properties, the RFC was scanned and analyzed by fluorescence, UV–vis and IR spectrometer, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 C, when excited at 425 nm, CuNCs displayed red fluorescence and a broad emission peak centered at 621 nm. This emission behavior was attributed to the cluster-centered (3CC) triplet excited states associated with the Cu3S2 center [46]. The observed emission properties of CuNCs underscored their potential applications in areas such as optoelectronics and sensing [47]. Additionally, when excited with a 331 nm wavelength, N-CQDs showed a peak emission at 421 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1 D. This suggested that the emission wavelength of N-CQDs depended on the Bohr radius of the carbonized exciton, highlighting the significance of the carbon structure in dictating their optical characteristics [28]. Fig. S9 showed the RFC with characteristic absorption peaks at 331 nm and 418 nm. Fig. S10 illustrated the fluorescence emission spectra of RFC when exposed to different excitation wavelengths. The optimal excitation wavelength was selected at 350 nm.

The ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was developed using Ce-UiO-66 and RFC. Scheme 1 outlined the experimental procedure for the synthesis and assembly of Ce-UiO-66 and RFC. In RFC, N-CQDs functioned as energy donors, while CuNCs served as energy acceptors. The fluorescence lifetime dynamics were investigated and presented in Fig. S11 and Table S1 . The presence of CuNCs reduced the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs from 7.45 ns to 1.66 ns, while simultaneously extending the fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs themselves from 0.73 ns to 1.68 ns. These findings substantiated the occurrence of FRET between CuNCs and N-CQDs, elucidating the phenomenon wherein the energy acceptor (CuNCs) modulated the behavior of the energy donor (N-CQDs).

Since Ce-UiO-66 could decompose MP into p-NP, the decomposition products would cause the blue emission signal of RFC to be quenched at 415 nm. The objective of this system was to detect the concentration of target samples through fluorescence emission. Several parameters were optimized to enhance the sensitivity and accuracy of the fluorescence detection assay. These parameters included (a) the usage of CuNCs, (b) pH value, (c) the usage of Ce-UiO-66, (d) temperature and (e) incubation time. Although the fluorescence quenching of RFC upon addition of 65 μg/mL and 130 μg/mL of CuNCs did not exhibit significant differences (p > 0.05) (Fig. 2
A), we opted to proceed with further experiments utilizing 130 μg/mL of CuNCs. This decision was motivated by the observation that RFC with 130 μg/mL of CuNCs displayed relatively strong fluorescence intensity with dual emission peaks, both of which exhibited similar intensities ( Fig. S12 ). This characteristic is expected to enhance the detection sensitivity of our experimental setup. Based on the data presented in Fig. 2 B-E, the optimal conditions for achieving maximum quenching efficiency and detection efficacy were established under pH 10, utilizing 5 mg of Ce-UiO-66, conducting measurements at a temperature of 65 °C, and incubating for a duration of 45 min. Accordingly, these parameters were selected as the optimal conditions for subsequent analyses. Under these conditions, the fluorescence intensity of blue emission signal was steady. However, upon the addition of p-NP, a quenching agent, the fluorescence intensity decreased (Fig. 2 F). The maximum fluorescence intensity was recorded at 415 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. This indicated the effective creation of a ratiometric fluorescent sensing system using Ce-UiO-66 and RFC, which offered potential applications in the detection and analysis of various samples. Under the same operation, the repeatability of the detection system for the same target was verified in a week. Fig. S13
A and B demonstrated that the probe exhibited high stability. As shown in Fig. S13
C, the quenching rate of MP detected by the established system remained stable with an RSD < 2.00 %, confirming the high repeatability of the system. We optimized the proportion of CuNCs in the detection system and the excitation wavelength, ensuring that the fluorescence intensity remained stable under different pH conditions ( Fig. S14 ) and over time.

The novel ratiometric fluorescence probe enabled swift identification of the specific pesticides. Upon introducing varying concentrations of the target analyte, MP, into the system, a corresponding fluorescence signal was recorded. Fig. 3
A showed that the fluorescence intensity at 415 nm, associated with blue emission signal, progressively diminished as the MP concentration increased. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity at 615 nm, related to blue emission signal, remained constant irrespective of the MP concentration. This finding indicated that the presence of MP had a minimal effect on the fluorescence emission of RFC in 615 nm. The concentration of MP was measured by calculating the quenching rate (QR) using the following equations.
QR = 1 - S
I 415
I 615
/ B
I 415
I 615

I415 and I615 denote the fluorescent intensity of RFC at 415 nm and 615 nm, respectively, with S representing samples containing MP and B representing those without MP. As the MP concentration increased from 1 to 100 μM, there was a notable quenching of the RFC fluorescence intensity at 415 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 B, two linear ranges of MP detection were observed due to the broad detection range. In the first range, from 1 to 40 μM, the correlation between fluorescence intensity and MP concentration was described by the equation Y = 0.01287X + 0.005156, with an R2 value of 0.9925. In the second range, from 40 to 100 μM, the relationship was described by Y = 0.004602X + 0.3293, with an R2 value of 0.9805. According to the three times signal-to-noise rule, the established detection method represented limit of detection (LOD) of 0.55 μM. The standard deviation of the measurements indicated the high precision and reliability of the system. Comparable performance was achieved in pollutant analysis, as demonstrated in Table S2 . These outcomes emphasized the effectiveness of the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe in the sensitive and precise detection of MP across a broad range of concentrations, making it a promising tool for various analytical applications.

The proposed detection system demonstrated its efficacy in overcoming the challenges posed by interfering substances commonly found in samples from natural plants. Accurate detection results were achieved despite the presence of common exogenous interferences. The anti-interference capability of the test system was evaluated by conducting tests on the detection system using a concentration of 80 μM of common OPs, including profenofos, chlorpyrifos, phosmet, methyl-bromophos, methyl-parathion, monocrotophos, phosalone, demeton, and methidadathion. At the same time, we tested the anti-interference ability of the sensing system in the presence of common metal ions or anions [48,49]. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 C–D, except for MP, the other 80 μM OPs and common ions were unable to produce a significant quenching effect on the RFC, indicating that the established method was highly specific and selective. The advantages of this detection mostly came from two aspects. The recognition and degradation of Ce-UiO-66 allowed OPs without corresponding structures to be excluded without being broken down into signaling molecules, and the multi-signal, ratiometric property of RFC, which effectively avoids internal effects and external interference.

As depicted in Fig. 2 F, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of p-NP coincided with the emission spectrum of RFC. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes were determined and were displayed in Fig. S15 and Table S3 . Adding MP or p-NP to RFC did not substantially alter the fluorescence lifetimes of N-CQDs and CuNCs within RFC. This suggested that a FRET process was not involved in the luminescence quenching observed. In summary, there was no energy transfer between RFC and p-NP, implying that the internal filtering effects may constituted the underlying detection mechanism for MP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of methyl-paraoxon in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The suitability of the proposed detection system was thoroughly evaluated by conducting tests on real samples. To assess performance of the system, recovery tests were carried out by spiking MP into various matrices, including edible plant like Coicis Semen and Ginseng Radix et Rhizoma, as well as tap water. The concentrations of MP in the spiked samples were determined, and the results were recorded in Table S4 . The analytical recovery rates were calculated to evaluate the accuracy of the detection system. As a result, the recoveries met the stringent requirements for detecting pesticide residues, as specified in the Chinese Pharmacopoeia 2020 edition, Part IV. The detection system proved to be highly reliable and effective in accurately detecting MP in real samples.

Visual pesticide detection has several advantages over other methods of detection. This method is non-invasive and non-destructive, making it suitable for detecting pesticides in real-world environments. Additionally, it is cost-effective and user-friendly [50]. There are several examples of visual pesticide detection methods [51–53]. In the present study, we explored and established a visual detection method for pesticide residue detection. Prior to detection, MP-spiked samples were incubated with Ce-UiO-66. As depicted in Fig. 4
A, as the concentration of MP increased from 0 to 800 μmol/L, a progressive color transition from transparent to yellow was observed. To achieve higher accuracy, the use of open-source software “Image J” facilitated the measurement of color chroma in MP-spiked samples. Using the “split channels” function of the software, the intensity of the blue channel was selected as a reference. This allowed for the determination of a linear relationship between the samples with different MP concentrations and the intensity of the blue channel, as illustrated in Fig. S16 . As demonstrated in Fig. 4 B and Fig. S17 , after adding different types of OPs and comparing their colors, it was found that only the group adding MP showed a yellow color, proving that the system represented high selectivity and good anti-interference ability.

In conclusion, the developed ratiometric fluorescent sensing system, employing Ce-UiO-66 and RFC, proved to be a highly effective and reliable tool for quantitative analysis. The system demonstrated high selectivity and sensitivity in the detection of MP as a fluorescent probe. This method exhibited good linearity across the MP concentration range of 1–100 μM, achieving a LOD of 0.55 μM. Validation tests conducted on edible plant and water samples confirmed the applicability of the method, demonstrating satisfactory recovery rates. This innovative sensing system showed great promise for practical applications in the analysis of real samples with complex metrics, contributing significantly to enhancing consumer safety and product quality standards.

Shaoyang Liu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Mai Luo: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Huizhen Liang: Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization, Data curation. Liang Zou: Resources, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Jiliang Cao: Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Jinchao Wei: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Peng Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We sincerely express our gratitude for the financial assistance provided by Macau Science and Technology Development Fund (0047/2020/AGJ, 0024/2021/A1, 0031/2022/AGJ and 005/2023/SKL), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2024A1515030214), as well as Research Committee of the University of Macau (MYRG2022-00226-ICMS, MYRG-CRG2022-00016-ICMS and SRG2022-00035-ICMS).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111781.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Pu, B. Wu, Y. Tan, Q. Wen, J. Ling, Q. Cao, Selective aggregation of silver nanoprisms induced by monohydrogen phosphate and its application for colorimetric detection of chromium (III) ions, J. Anal. Test. 5 (2021) 225-234, 10.1007/s41664-021-00183-y.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Origami multiple paper-based electrochemical biosensors for pesticide detection
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Arduini, S. Cinti, V. Caratelli, L. Amendola, G. Palleschi, D. Moscone, Origami multiple paper-based electrochemical biosensors for pesticide detection, Biosens. Bioelectron. 126 (2019) 346-354, 10.1016/j.bios.2018.10.014.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on hydrogen-bond triggering the internal filter effect for enzyme-free and visual monitoring pesticide residues
ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Qin, Y. Guo, L. Li, D. Lin, Y. Li, S. Xu, C. Jiang, Ratiometric Fluorescent Sensor Based on Hydrogen-Bond Triggering the Internal Filter Effect for Enzyme-Free and Visual Monitoring Pesticide Residues, ACS Sustainable Chem. Eng. 11 (2023) 11032-11040, 10.1021/acssuschemeng.3c00772.
